symbols.name: helix_bioage
symbols.doc: # helix-bioage — ADR-034: biological-age estimate (Levine PhenoAge)

symbols.name: PhenoInputs
symbols.signature: struct PhenoInputs { albumin_g_l: f64, creatinine_umol_l: f64, glucose_mmol_l: f64, crp_mg_dl: f64, lymphocyte_pct: f64, mcv_fl: f64, rdw_pct: f64, alk_phosphatase_u_l: f64, wbc_1000_ul: f64, age_years: f64 }
symbols.doc: The 9 routine biomarkers + chronological age PhenoAge requires, **in the units the algorithm expects** (documented per field — unit mistakes are the main failure mode).

symbols.name: BioAge
symbols.signature: struct BioAge { phenoage_years: f64, chronological_years: f64, delta_years: f64 }
symbols.doc: A biological-age estimate. The `delta_years` (PhenoAge − chronological) is the honest headline; `phenoage_years` is the absolute estimate.

symbols.name: phenoage
symbols.doc: Compute the PhenoAge biological-age estimate. Validates each input is finite and within a generous physiological range (abstain otherwise, ADR-006).

symbols.name: to_record
symbols.signature: fn to_record(b: &BioAge, measured_at: EpochMillis, source_file: &str) -> ProvRecord
symbols.doc: Render the estimate as a derived, provenance-tagged record (ADR-005). Code is `BIOAGE-PHENOAGE` (never a clinical LOINC, ADR-004); confidence is capped — this is a population-derived estimate, not a measurement.

symbols.name: DISCLAIMER
symbols.doc: The framing that must accompany every biological-age estimate (ADR-034/010).

symbols.name: helix_cohort
symbols.doc: # helix-cohort — ADR-024: privacy-preserving cohort feature extraction

symbols.name: GeneralizedFeature
symbols.signature: struct GeneralizedFeature { key: String, value: f64, cohort_cell_size: u32, quasi_identifier: bool }
symbols.doc: A generalized (already-binned) feature ready for the privacy gate. The caller is responsible for never putting a raw value, date, id, or free text here.

symbols.name: CohortConfig
symbols.signature: struct CohortConfig { epsilon: f64, k_threshold: u32, suppressed_keys: Vec<String> }
symbols.doc: Policy config for the privacy gate.

symbols.name: CohortVector
symbols.signature: struct CohortVector { features: Vec<(String, f64)>, epsilon_spent: f64, suppressed: Vec<String> }
symbols.doc: A contribution-safe cohort vector: noised band values + the ε actually spent.

symbols.name: NoiseSource
symbols.doc: Source of uniform samples in `(-0.5, 0.5)` for the Laplace mechanism. Production: CSPRNG. Tests: a deterministic stub.

symbols.name: generalize
symbols.signature: fn generalize(features: &[GeneralizedFeature], cfg: &CohortConfig, noise: &mut dyn _) -> Result<CohortVector, CohortError>
symbols.doc: Run the privacy gate. Returns a contribution-safe [`CohortVector`], or [`CohortError::NothingSafeToShare`] if no feature survives.

symbols.name: helix_connect
symbols.doc: # helix-connect — ADR-029: connector clients (FHIR/SMART + wearables)

symbols.name: HttpTransport
symbols.doc: Abstracts the network. Production: a real HTTP client + per-provider auth. Tests: a sandbox returning canned payloads. `get` returns the response body.

symbols.name: get
symbols.signature: fn get(self: &Self, url: &str, bearer: Option<&str>) -> Result<String, ConnectError>

symbols.name: ConnectError
symbols.signature: enum ConnectError { Transport, Unauthorized, RateLimited, Unavailable, Parse }

symbols.name: OAuthToken
symbols.signature: struct OAuthToken { access_token: String, refresh_token: Option<String>, expires_at: EpochMillis, scopes: Vec<String> }
symbols.doc: An OAuth token (SMART on FHIR / wearable). **Secret** — never logged; held only in the user's vault (ADR-001).

symbols.name: OAuthTokenRedacted
symbols.doc: Wrapper to print a token without leaking it (for logs/audit).

symbols.name: FallbackTier
symbols.signature: enum FallbackTier { LiveApi, UserExport, PdfOcr, Manual }
symbols.doc: The fallback tier a connector drops to when the live API can't serve (ADR-012).

symbols.name: fallback_for
symbols.doc: Map a live-API failure to the next degradation tier (ADR-012 made executable).

symbols.name: parse_observation
symbols.doc: Parse a FHIR R4 `Observation` JSON value into a [`ProvRecord`]. Extracts LOINC code (ADR-004), value + UCUM unit, effective date, reference range, and source. Un-parseable → `ConnectError::Parse` (→ review queue).

symbols.name: FhirConnector
symbols.signature: struct FhirConnector { transport: &T, base_url: String, source: String }
symbols.doc: A connector: fetch FHIR observations for a concept, parsing them into records, or report the fallback tier on failure (ADR-012 degradation).

symbols.name: PullResult
symbols.doc: Outcome of a connector pull.

symbols.name: new
symbols.signature: FhirConnector::fn new(transport: &T, base_url: impl _, source: impl _) -> Self

symbols.name: new
symbols.signature: fn new(transport: &T, base_url: impl _, source: impl _) -> Self

symbols.name: pull_observations
symbols.signature: FhirConnector::fn pull_observations(self: &Self, loinc: &str, token: &OAuthToken, now: EpochMillis) -> PullResult
symbols.doc: Pull `Observation`s for a LOINC code, refreshing nothing here (the caller supplies a valid token). On transport failure, degrade per ADR-012.

symbols.name: pull_observations
symbols.signature: fn pull_observations(self: &Self, loinc: &str, token: &OAuthToken, now: EpochMillis) -> PullResult
symbols.doc: Pull `Observation`s for a LOINC code, refreshing nothing here (the caller supplies a valid token). On transport failure, degrade per ADR-012.

symbols.name: parse_apple_health
symbols.signature: fn parse_apple_health(xml: &str, source: &str, max_records: usize) -> Vec<ProvRecord>
symbols.doc: Parse an Apple Health `export.xml` string into provenance records. Scans `<Record ...>` elements, maps known HealthKit types (ADR-004), and skips unknown/invalid ones. Bounded by `max_records` to avoid huge exports blowing memory.

symbols.name: helix_embed
symbols.doc: # helix-embed — ADR-027: learned MiniLM text embeddings (local GPU)

symbols.name: TextEmbedder
symbols.doc: Produces a fixed-dimension embedding for a piece of text.

symbols.name: cosine
symbols.doc: Cosine similarity between two equal-length vectors. Returns 0 for empty or mismatched inputs (safe-degradation contract).

symbols.name: record_text
symbols.doc: Compact, provenance-preserving rendering of a record for embedding. Never adds anything the record doesn't already hold.

symbols.name: LocalEmbedder
symbols.signature: struct LocalEmbedder { url: String, model: String, timeout_secs: u64 }
symbols.doc: Local-GPU embeddings backend (OpenAI-/ollama-style embeddings endpoint). Default: all-MiniLM-L6-v2 served by ollama on the GPU.

symbols.name: LearnedEmbedder
symbols.doc: Adapts a [`TextEmbedder`] to `helix_retrieval::Embedder`. On backend failure it returns an empty vector (no spurious matches) so one embedding error never fails the whole answer.

symbols.name: helix_escalation
symbols.doc: # helix-escalation — ADR-009: Red-Flag Escalation & Clinician-in-the-Loop

symbols.name: EscalationLevel
symbols.doc: Severity of an escalation. Both non-`None` levels suppress optimization.

symbols.name: Grade
symbols.doc: Whether a threshold is screening-grade (ambient sensing) or a measured clinical value. Screening flags never use diagnostic language.

symbols.name: RedFlagThreshold
symbols.signature: struct RedFlagThreshold { code: String, concept: String, unit: String, grade: Grade, low_critical: Option<f64>, low_urgent: Option<f64>, high_urgent: Option<f64>, high_critical: Option<f64>, source: String }
symbols.doc: One red-flag rule for a concept, keyed by its canonical code (LOINC etc.). Bounds are optional so one-sided flags (e.g. SpO₂ only-low) are expressible.

symbols.name: level_for
symbols.doc: Evaluate a single value against this rule. Critical bounds dominate urgent bounds; low/high are checked independently.

symbols.name: level_for
symbols.doc: Evaluate a single value against this rule. Critical bounds dominate urgent bounds; low/high are checked independently.

symbols.name: EscalationResult
symbols.signature: struct EscalationResult { level: EscalationLevel, suppress_optimization: bool, message: String }
symbols.doc: The full evaluation outcome for a value.

symbols.name: ThresholdRegistry
symbols.signature: struct ThresholdRegistry { version: String, thresholds: Vec<RedFlagThreshold> }
symbols.doc: A versioned set of thresholds. The version string is part of the audit trail and changes only via clinical governance — never Darwin Mode.

symbols.name: evaluate
symbols.signature: ThresholdRegistry::fn evaluate(self: &Self, code: &str, value: f64) -> Result<EscalationResult, EscalationError>
symbols.doc: Evaluate a measured value for a concept. Unknown codes are an explicit error (we never silently treat an un-modelled value as safe).

symbols.name: evaluate
symbols.signature: fn evaluate(self: &Self, code: &str, value: f64) -> Result<EscalationResult, EscalationError>
symbols.doc: Evaluate a measured value for a concept. Unknown codes are an explicit error (we never silently treat an un-modelled value as safe).

symbols.name: builtin_registry_v1
symbols.doc: The built-in v1 registry. Numbers cite their clinical source; these are the starting point a medical advisory board ratifies and versions (ADR-009).

symbols.name: helix_evidence
symbols.doc: # helix-evidence — ADR-006: Evidence Tiering & Explicit Abstention Policy

symbols.name: EvidenceTier
symbols.signature: enum EvidenceTier { YourData, ReferenceStandard, PeerReviewed, Heuristic }
symbols.doc: Strength-of-support label, mapped onto the standard evidence hierarchy of evidence-based medicine (Oxford CEBM): Tier 1 is the user's own measured data; Tier 4 is heuristic "biohacker lore" that must never be dressed up as established fact.

symbols.name: label
symbols.doc: Short human label for the UI chip.

symbols.name: label
symbols.doc: Short human label for the UI chip.

symbols.name: is_low_evidence
symbols.doc: Tier 4 must always be visibly flagged as low-evidence.

symbols.name: is_low_evidence
symbols.doc: Tier 4 must always be visibly flagged as low-evidence.

symbols.name: TieredRecommendation
symbols.signature: struct TieredRecommendation { text: String, tier: EvidenceTier }
symbols.doc: A recommendation carrying its evidence tier. Constructing one *requires* a tier — there is no untiered recommendation in Helix.

symbols.name: new
symbols.signature: TieredRecommendation::fn new(text: impl _, tier: EvidenceTier) -> Self

symbols.name: new
symbols.signature: fn new(text: impl _, tier: EvidenceTier) -> Self

symbols.name: AbstentionReason
symbols.doc: Why Helix declined to answer about a concept. Abstaining is a feature, not a failure — each reason maps to a specific, honest [`GapNotice`].

symbols.name: GapNotice
symbols.signature: struct GapNotice { reason: AbstentionReason, message: String, suggested_action: String }
symbols.doc: What the user sees when Helix abstains: an honest, non-apologetic note that always offers a way forward (never a dead end).

symbols.name: AnswerVerdict
symbols.doc: Outcome of [`assess`]: either Helix may answer, or it must abstain with a gap notice.

symbols.name: DataKind
symbols.signature: enum DataKind { LipidPanel, Ferritin, Hormone, BloodPressure, AmbientVital, WearableDaily }
symbols.doc: Default staleness windows (days) for common data kinds. These are policy, not physics — versioned and reviewable.

symbols.name: assess
symbols.signature: fn assess(now: EpochMillis, latest: Option<&ProvRecord>, window_days: i64, confidence_floor: f64) -> Result<AnswerVerdict, EvidenceError>
symbols.doc: The abstention gate. Given the current time, the most recent record for a concept (if any), a staleness window and a confidence floor, decide whether Helix may answer or must abstain.

symbols.name: helix_evolve
symbols.doc: # helix-evolve — ADR-035: Darwin-style parameter evolution (safety-frozen)

symbols.name: Params
symbols.signature: struct Params { confidence_floor: f64, staleness_window_days: i64, flat_band_per_day: f64, flat_band_frac: f64 }
symbols.doc: The tunable, **non-safety** parameters Darwin may evolve.

symbols.name: Bounds
symbols.signature: struct Bounds { confidence_floor: (f64, f64), staleness_window_days: (i64, i64), flat_band_per_day: (f64, f64), flat_band_frac: (f64, f64) }
symbols.doc: Inclusive bounds for the search space — evolution never leaves these, so it can't drive a parameter to a degenerate value.

symbols.name: Expected
symbols.doc: The ground-truth behavior a case should elicit.

symbols.name: EvalCase
symbols.signature: struct EvalCase { name: String, concept_code: String, records: Vec<ProvRecord>, now: EpochMillis, reference_low: Option<f64>, reference_high: Option<f64>, expected: Expected }
symbols.doc: One labeled evaluation case — records plus the known-correct behavior.

symbols.name: Fitness
symbols.signature: struct Fitness { score: f64, grounded_correct: u32, abstained_correct: u32, over_confident: u32, over_cautious: u32, wrong_direction: u32 }
symbols.doc: Fitness breakdown over an eval set. `score` is the grounding-first total.

symbols.name: fitness
symbols.doc: Score a parameter set against the eval cases. The `registry` (red-flag thresholds) is **frozen** — passed in, never mutated.

symbols.name: EvolveResult
symbols.signature: struct EvolveResult { seed_params: Params, seed_fitness: Fitness, best_params: Params, best_fitness: Fitness, trajectory: Vec<f64> }
symbols.doc: The result of an evolve run.

symbols.name: improvement
symbols.doc: How much fitness improved (final − baseline).

symbols.name: improvement
symbols.doc: How much fitness improved (final − baseline).

symbols.name: evolve
symbols.signature: fn evolve(seed_params: Params, bounds: &Bounds, cases: &[EvalCase], registry: &ThresholdRegistry, generations: usize, seed: u64) -> EvolveResult
symbols.doc: Evolve [`Params`] by seeded hill-climbing: each generation proposes a mutation, evaluates it against the eval set, and **keeps it only if fitness improves** (the Darwin invariant). Deterministic for a given `seed`.

symbols.name: helix_fed
symbols.doc: # helix-fed — ADR-030: federation transport (opt-in, DP-gated)

symbols.name: Consent
symbols.signature: struct Consent { scope: String, expires_at: i64 }
symbols.doc: Per-contribution consent (opt-in, expiring). Without it, contribution refuses.

symbols.name: is_valid
symbols.signature: Consent::fn is_valid(self: &Self, now: i64, scope: &str) -> bool

symbols.name: is_valid
symbols.signature: fn is_valid(self: &Self, now: i64, scope: &str) -> bool

symbols.name: Contribution
symbols.signature: struct Contribution { features: Vec<(String, f64)>, epsilon_spent: f64, scope: String }
symbols.doc: The envelope that crosses the wire — a DP-noised vector and the ε it cost. Constructed only from a [`CohortVector`]; carries nothing else.

symbols.name: CohortSignal
symbols.signature: struct CohortSignal { cohort_size: u32, aggregate: f64, epsilon_spent: f64 }
symbols.doc: An aggregate signal returned from the cohort — never an individual's data.

symbols.name: FedError
symbols.signature: enum FedError { NoConsent, GenomicExcluded, CohortTooSmall, Transport }

symbols.name: make_contribution
symbols.doc: Build a [`Contribution`] from a gated [`CohortVector`]. This is the **only** constructor — raw data has no path to the wire. Re-asserts the genomics exclusion (defense in depth on top of ADR-024).

symbols.name: FedTransport
symbols.doc: Abstracts the federation network. Tests: an in-memory aggregator. Production: a signed-dispatch transport (Ruflo federation, Ed25519).

symbols.name: submit
symbols.doc: Submit a contribution and request the matching cohort signal.

symbols.name: FederationClient
symbols.doc: The federation client. `contribute` enforces consent + the type-level privacy gate before anything leaves the device.

symbols.name: contribute
symbols.signature: FederationClient::fn contribute(self: &Self, vector: &CohortVector, consent: &Consent, scope: &str, now: i64) -> Result<CohortSignal, FedError>
symbols.doc: Contribute a gated cohort vector under explicit consent, returning the aggregate cohort signal. Refuses without valid consent; only a `CohortVector` can be passed (privacy gate is the only door).

symbols.name: contribute
symbols.signature: fn contribute(self: &Self, vector: &CohortVector, consent: &Consent, scope: &str, now: i64) -> Result<CohortSignal, FedError>
symbols.doc: Contribute a gated cohort vector under explicit consent, returning the aggregate cohort signal. Refuses without valid consent; only a `CohortVector` can be passed (privacy gate is the only door).

symbols.name: helix_focus
symbols.doc: # helix-focus — ADR-032: evidence-based "focus areas" (non-diagnostic)

symbols.name: FocusReason
symbols.doc: Why a concept was surfaced as a focus area.

symbols.name: Severity
symbols.doc: Severity for ranking (not a clinical grade).

symbols.name: FocusItem
symbols.signature: struct FocusItem { concept: String, reason: FocusReason, severity: Severity, message: String, cites: Vec<String> }
symbols.doc: One focus item — "worth attention", with its reason, citations, and a non-diagnostic message.

symbols.name: FocusConfig
symbols.signature: struct FocusConfig { trend_band_per_day: f64, stale_days: i64, critical_codes: Vec<String> }
symbols.doc: Config for focus selection.

symbols.name: select_focus
symbols.signature: fn select_focus(records: &[ProvRecord], now: EpochMillis, cfg: &FocusConfig) -> Vec<FocusItem>
symbols.doc: Select focus areas from records grouped by concept code. `now` is supplied (deterministic, ADR-007). Returns items ranked by severity then recency.

symbols.name: helix_genome
symbols.doc: # helix-genome — ADR-021: user-owned genome ingestion & pharmacogenomics

symbols.name: GENOME_PRIVACY_NOTE
symbols.doc: Standing privacy note attached to all genomic-derived signals.

symbols.name: GENOME_DISCLAIMER
symbols.doc: Non-diagnostic framing for genomic risk / pharmacogenomics.

symbols.name: Metabolizer
symbols.signature: enum Metabolizer { Poor, Intermediate, Normal, Rapid, Ultrarapid }
symbols.doc: Metabolizer phenotype from a pharmacogenomic star-allele diplotype.

symbols.name: needs_advisory
symbols.doc: Normal metabolizers need no advisory; everything else is worth a prescriber conversation (dosing of common drugs can change).

symbols.name: needs_advisory
symbols.doc: Normal metabolizers need no advisory; everything else is worth a prescriber conversation (dosing of common drugs can change).

symbols.name: PharmacoCall
symbols.signature: struct PharmacoCall { gene: String, diplotype: String, phenotype: Metabolizer }
symbols.doc: A pharmacogenomic call: a gene, its diplotype, and the inferred phenotype.

symbols.name: BiomarkerRisk
symbols.signature: struct BiomarkerRisk { trait_name: String, score: f64, band: String }
symbols.doc: A biomarker / polygenic risk result with its band and caveat.

symbols.name: GenomeProfile
symbols.signature: struct GenomeProfile { source_file: String, imported_at: EpochMillis, genotype_count: u32, pharmaco: Vec<PharmacoCall>, risks: Vec<BiomarkerRisk>, ancestry_caveat: Option<String> }
symbols.doc: A derived genomic profile from rvDNA, computed on the user's own file.

symbols.name: PrescriberAdvisory
symbols.signature: struct PrescriberAdvisory { gene: String, phenotype: Metabolizer, message: String }
symbols.doc: A "verify with your prescriber" advisory derived from a pharmacogenomic call. Decision-support only — never a dosing directive (ADR-010).

symbols.name: profile_to_records
symbols.doc: Map a genome profile into provenance records: one per pharmacogenomic phenotype and one per biomarker risk. Genomic data is the most sensitive, so every record is `Derived`/`GENO-*` at capped confidence.

symbols.name: prescriber_advisories
symbols.doc: Produce prescriber advisories for any non-normal metabolizer phenotype. These surface when relevant medications are in the dossier — as prompts to verify, never as directives.

symbols.name: SnpFinding
symbols.signature: struct SnpFinding { rsid: String, gene: String, label: String, genotype: String, effect_allele_copies: u8, note: String }
symbols.doc: A finding from an annotated SNP.

symbols.name: RawGenomeResult
symbols.signature: struct RawGenomeResult { total_variants: u32, records: Vec<ProvRecord>, findings: Vec<SnpFinding>, caveat: String }
symbols.doc: Result of importing a 23andMe raw genotype file.

symbols.name: parse_23andme_raw
symbols.doc: Parse a 23andMe-style raw genotype file (`rsid\tchrom\tpos\tgenotype`, `#` comments). The raw file stays user-owned and local (§7.4/ADR-001); this only surfaces a few well-documented single-variant findings, **not** a full pharmacogenomic diplotype call.

symbols.name: helix_llm
symbols.doc: # helix-llm — ADR-026: on-device LLM analyst (grounded compose step)

symbols.name: SYSTEM_PROMPT
symbols.doc: The strict narrator instruction (ADR-026 §1, §3).

symbols.name: LlmBackend
symbols.doc: A pluggable chat backend. Production: [`LocalLlmBackend`] on the local GPU.

symbols.name: complete
symbols.signature: fn complete(self: &Self, system: &str, user: &str) -> Result<String, LlmError>

symbols.name: Composed
symbols.signature: struct Composed { text: String, used_llm: bool, guard_rejected: Option<String> }
symbols.doc: Result of a compose: the prose, whether the LLM output was used or the deterministic fallback kicked in, and any guard rejection reason.

symbols.name: compose
symbols.doc: Compose a grounded answer into prose. Calls the backend, then enforces the number-guard; on any guard rejection or backend error, returns the safe deterministic template.

symbols.name: LocalLlmBackend
symbols.signature: struct LocalLlmBackend { base_url: String, model: String, timeout_secs: u64 }
symbols.doc: Local-GPU backend: any OpenAI-compatible chat endpoint, on-device. Temperature 0 for reproducible narration. No health data leaves the device (ADR-013).

symbols.name: ruvllm
symbols.doc: In-stack ruvLLM endpoint (ruvnet-native, ADR-013).

symbols.name: ruvllm
symbols.doc: In-stack ruvLLM endpoint (ruvnet-native, ADR-013).

symbols.name: ollama
symbols.doc: Ollama fallback endpoint.

symbols.name: ollama
symbols.doc: Ollama fallback endpoint.

symbols.name: default
symbols.doc: ruvLLM — the in-stack on-device engine (ADR-013).

symbols.name: default
symbols.doc: ruvLLM — the in-stack on-device engine (ADR-013).

symbols.name: complete
symbols.signature: LocalLlmBackend::fn complete(self: &Self, system: &str, user: &str) -> Result<String, LlmError>

symbols.name: complete
symbols.signature: fn complete(self: &Self, system: &str, user: &str) -> Result<String, LlmError>

symbols.name: helix_neural
symbols.doc: # helix-neural — integration adapter for `ruvnet/ruv-neural`

symbols.name: RESEARCH_DISCLAIMER
symbols.doc: The framing that must accompany any neuro-derived signal in the UI.

symbols.name: SessionMetric
symbols.signature: struct SessionMetric { key: String, value: f64, unit: String }
symbols.doc: One metric measured during a ruv-neural session.

symbols.name: NeuralSession
symbols.signature: struct NeuralSession { session_id: String, recorded_at: EpochMillis, protocol: String, duration_s: u32, metrics: Vec<SessionMetric>, evidence_signature: String }
symbols.doc: A ruv-neural session's signed evidence, as exported by the harness.

symbols.name: session_to_records
symbols.doc: Convert a signed ruv-neural session into provenance-tagged Helix records — one per metric. Refuses unsigned or empty/non-finite sessions (a record with no provenance is not allowed into the graph, ADR-005).

symbols.name: neuro_orientation
symbols.doc: A 0–100 "Neuro" orientation value for the health-score's optional Neuro subsystem (ADR-016), derived only from entrainment quality metrics. This is an *orientation* number, explicitly not a clinical score; returns `None` when no entrainment metric is present.

symbols.name: helix_numeric
symbols.doc: # helix-numeric — ADR-007: Deterministic Numeric/Trend Engine

symbols.name: EpochMillis
symbols.doc: Milliseconds since the Unix epoch (caller-supplied; the engine never reads the wall clock).

symbols.name: Point
symbols.signature: struct Point { t: EpochMillis, value: f64 }
symbols.doc: One observation in a series.

symbols.name: new
symbols.signature: Point::fn new(t: EpochMillis, value: f64) -> Self

symbols.name: new
symbols.signature: fn new(t: EpochMillis, value: f64) -> Self

symbols.name: NumericError
symbols.signature: enum NumericError { TooFewPoints, Unordered, NonFinite, ZeroTimeSpan }
symbols.doc: Why a computation refused to run. Returning an error here is a *feature*: it is how the engine declines to manufacture a statistic the data cannot support, which the analyst then surfaces as an ADR-006 abstention rather than a guess.

symbols.name: validate
symbols.doc: Validates a series is finite and strictly time-ordered. Most engine functions call this first so callers get one consistent contract.

symbols.name: mean
symbols.doc: Arithmetic mean of the values. Minimum sample size: 1.

symbols.name: delta
symbols.doc: Absolute change from the first to the last observation. Minimum: 2 points.

symbols.name: percent_change
symbols.doc: Percent change from first to last observation, as a fraction (0.10 == +10%). Returns `None`-equivalent error semantics via [`NumericError::NonFinite`] when the baseline is zero (percent change is undefined). Minimum: 2 points.

symbols.name: slope_per_day
symbols.doc: Ordinary least-squares slope of value vs. time, in **units per day**. Positive == trending up. Minimum: 2 points; errors if all timestamps equal.

symbols.name: RangePosition
symbols.doc: Where a value sits against a reference interval (mirrors `helix-provenance::RangePosition`; duplicated to keep this crate dependency free at the leaf).

symbols.name: RangeCrossing
symbols.signature: struct RangeCrossing { at: EpochMillis, from: RangePosition, to: RangePosition }
symbols.doc: A point at which the series crossed a reference bound between consecutive observations.

symbols.name: range_crossings
symbols.signature: fn range_crossings(series: &[Point], low: Option<f64>, high: Option<f64>) -> Result<Vec<RangeCrossing>, NumericError>
symbols.doc: Detects every reference-range crossing in the series. Minimum: 2 points.

symbols.name: pearson
symbols.doc: Pearson correlation between two equal-length, index-aligned series of values. Minimum: 3 pairs (below that, correlation is not meaningful). Returns a value in `[-1.0, 1.0]`.

symbols.name: ChangePoint
symbols.signature: struct ChangePoint { index: usize, at: EpochMillis, mean_before: f64, mean_after: f64 }
symbols.doc: A detected change-point: the index/timestamp where the running mean shifted most sharply, per a simple CUSUM-style scan.

symbols.name: change_point
symbols.doc: Detects the single most significant change-point via the maximum absolute CUSUM deviation from the global mean. Minimum: 4 points (need ≥2 on each side of a split). Returns `Ok(None)` when no interior split improves on a flat series.

symbols.name: TrendDirection
symbols.doc: A direction label for narrating a trend without the analyst doing math.

symbols.name: trend_direction
symbols.signature: fn trend_direction(slope_per_day: f64, flat_band: f64) -> TrendDirection
symbols.doc: Classifies a slope into a direction using a caller-supplied dead-band (units/day below which the trend is reported as flat — prevents over-reading noise as a trend).

symbols.name: trend_direction_relative
symbols.signature: fn trend_direction_relative(slope_per_day: f64, range_span: f64, window_days: f64, frac: f64) -> TrendDirection
symbols.doc: Scale-invariant trend classification: a move counts as a trend only if it exceeds `frac` of the reference-range span over the observation window. This makes one threshold work across markers of very different scales — a single absolute `flat_band` cannot (a slow HbA1c rise and ch

symbols.name: helix_ocr
symbols.doc: # helix-ocr — ADR-022: OCR lab-document ingestion

symbols.name: OcrCandidate
symbols.signature: struct OcrCandidate { label: String, value: f64, unit: String, reference_low: Option<f64>, reference_high: Option<f64>, ocr_confidence: f64 }
symbols.doc: A raw candidate analyte as extracted from a lab document by OCR.

symbols.name: Plausible
symbols.signature: struct Plausible { min: f64, max: f64 }
symbols.doc: A coarse plausibility band for an analyte (outer bounds; anything outside is almost certainly an OCR error, not a real reading).

symbols.name: QueueReason
symbols.signature: enum QueueReason { LowOcrConfidence, NonFinite, MissingUnit, Implausible }
symbols.doc: Why a candidate was sent to review instead of accepted.

symbols.name: Gated
symbols.doc: The gate outcome for one candidate.

symbols.name: OcrDocument
symbols.signature: struct OcrDocument { doc_label: String, imported_at: EpochMillis, candidates: Vec<OcrCandidate> }
symbols.doc: Lab document the OCR engine processed, plus when it was imported.

symbols.name: gate_candidate
symbols.signature: fn gate_candidate(c: &OcrCandidate, doc_label: &str, imported_at: EpochMillis, plausible: Option<Plausible>, floor: f64) -> Result<Gated, OcrError>
symbols.doc: Gate one candidate. `plausible` is the optional coarse range for this analyte; `floor` is the minimum OCR confidence to accept. The accepted record's confidence is the OCR confidence **capped at 0.8** (never as trusted as a structured feed).

symbols.name: ingest_document
symbols.signature: fn ingest_document(doc: &OcrDocument, floor: f64, plausible_for: F) -> Result<Vec<Gated>, OcrError>
symbols.doc: Gate every candidate in a document. `plausible_for` supplies the coarse range per analyte label (return `None` for analytes without a curated band).

symbols.name: helix_ontology
symbols.doc: # helix-ontology — ADR-004: Canonical Ontology Normalization

symbols.name: CodeSystem
symbols.signature: enum CodeSystem { Loinc, RxNorm, SnomedCt, Icd10Cm, Ucum }
symbols.doc: The canonical code systems Helix normalizes to. Each carries its FHIR system URI so a normalized concept round-trips into a FHIR `Coding`.

symbols.name: fhir_uri
symbols.doc: The canonical FHIR `system` URI for this code system.

symbols.name: fhir_uri
symbols.doc: The canonical FHIR `system` URI for this code system.

symbols.name: domain
symbols.doc: Which clinical domain this system is the canonical choice for.

symbols.name: domain
symbols.doc: Which clinical domain this system is the canonical choice for.

symbols.name: Domain
symbols.signature: enum Domain { Observation, Medication, Condition, Unit }
symbols.doc: Clinical domain of an incoming datum, used to pick the target code system.

symbols.name: canonical_system
symbols.doc: The canonical code system Helix maps this domain to (ADR-004).

symbols.name: canonical_system
symbols.doc: The canonical code system Helix maps this domain to (ADR-004).

symbols.name: CanonicalCode
symbols.signature: struct CanonicalCode { system: CodeSystem, code: String, display: String }
symbols.doc: A successfully normalized concept: a canonical code plus a FHIR-ready coding.

symbols.name: new
symbols.signature: CanonicalCode::fn new(system: CodeSystem, code: impl _, display: impl _) -> Self

symbols.name: new
symbols.signature: fn new(system: CodeSystem, code: impl _, display: impl _) -> Self

symbols.name: to_fhir_coding
symbols.doc: Render as a FHIR `Coding` JSON object (interchange model).

symbols.name: to_fhir_coding
symbols.doc: Render as a FHIR `Coding` JSON object (interchange model).

symbols.name: RawTerm
symbols.signature: struct RawTerm { text: String, domain: Domain, unit: Option<String> }
symbols.doc: A raw, un-normalized term as it arrived from a source, with whatever hint the connector could attach.

symbols.name: NormalizationOutcome
symbols.doc: The outcome of normalizing one [`RawTerm`]. A confident match is normalized; anything uncertain is routed to review — never silently coerced (ADR-004).

symbols.name: ReviewItem
symbols.signature: struct ReviewItem { raw: RawTerm, reason: ReviewReason, candidates: Vec<ScoredCandidate> }
symbols.doc: An item parked in the human-review queue, with enough context for a curator to resolve it. Nothing here ever reaches the analytic graph until resolved.

symbols.name: ScoredCandidate
symbols.signature: struct ScoredCandidate { canonical: CanonicalCode, score: f64 }

symbols.name: normalize
symbols.signature: fn normalize(raw: &RawTerm, candidates: Vec<ScoredCandidate>, floor: f64, ambiguity_margin: f64) -> Result<NormalizationOutcome, OntologyError>
symbols.doc: Normalize a raw term given a ranked candidate list (produced upstream by the matcher: exact code lookup → fuzzy NLP). Policy, not matching, lives here:

symbols.name: helix_pipeline
symbols.doc: # helix-pipeline — the grounded-answer pipeline (ADR-002 orchestration of ADR-005/006/007/009)

symbols.name: AnalyzeRequest
symbols.signature: struct AnalyzeRequest { concept_code: &str, records: &[ProvRecord], now: EpochMillis, staleness_window_days: i64, confidence_floor: f64, reference_low: Option<f64>, reference_high: Option<f64>, flat_band_per_day: f64, flat_band_frac: f64 }
symbols.doc: Everything the pipeline needs to answer a single concept question. Borrowed so the caller keeps ownership of the vault records.

symbols.name: TrendFacts
symbols.signature: struct TrendFacts { latest_value: f64, latest_at: EpochMillis, mean: f64, slope_per_day: Option<f64>, direction: TrendDirection, percent_change: Option<f64>, crossings: Vec<RangeCrossing>, sample_size: usize }
symbols.doc: Deterministically-computed trend facts (ADR-007 output handed to the analyst).

symbols.name: GroundedAnswer
symbols.signature: struct GroundedAnswer { escalation: EscalationResult, claims: Vec<GroundedClaim>, trend: TrendFacts, recommendation: Option<TieredRecommendation> }
symbols.doc: A full grounded answer. `escalation.level == None` in the normal case.

symbols.name: AnswerOutcome
symbols.doc: The pipeline outcome: either an honest abstention or a grounded answer.

symbols.name: analyze
symbols.doc: Run the full grounded-answer pipeline for one concept.

symbols.name: helix_provenance
symbols.doc: # helix-provenance — ADR-005: Retrieval-Grounded, Provenance-Required Answering

symbols.name: EpochMillis
symbols.doc: Milliseconds since the Unix epoch. Helix never reads the wall clock inside pure logic — timestamps are always passed in by the caller, which keeps every computation reproducible (ADR-007 principle, applied here).

symbols.name: MeasurementMethod
symbols.signature: enum MeasurementMethod { LabFeed, OcrExtraction, Device, AmbientSensing, ManualEntry, Derived }
symbols.doc: How a datum entered the vault. Used by the numeric/trend engine to decide whether two values are directly comparable (e.g. you do not trend a manual entry against a lab-feed value without flagging the method change).

symbols.name: ReferenceRange
symbols.signature: struct ReferenceRange { low: Option<f64>, high: Option<f64> }
symbols.doc: A closed reference interval `[low, high]` in the record's units. Either bound may be open (`None`) for one-sided ranges (e.g. "troponin < 0.04").

symbols.name: new
symbols.signature: ReferenceRange::fn new(low: Option<f64>, high: Option<f64>) -> Self

symbols.name: new
symbols.signature: fn new(low: Option<f64>, high: Option<f64>) -> Self

symbols.name: classify
symbols.doc: Where a value sits relative to the range.

symbols.name: classify
symbols.doc: Where a value sits relative to the range.

symbols.name: RangePosition
symbols.doc: Result of comparing a value against its [`ReferenceRange`].

symbols.name: Confidence
symbols.doc: Calibrated confidence in a single datum, `0.0..=1.0`. Distinct from evidence tier (ADR-006): tier is *what kind* of support; confidence is *how solid* this particular measurement is (e.g. an OCR extraction with a low parser score carries lower confidence than a structured lab fee

symbols.name: new
symbols.doc: Clamps into `0.0..=1.0`; never panics.

symbols.name: new
symbols.doc: Clamps into `0.0..=1.0`; never panics.

symbols.name: RecordId
symbols.doc: Stable identifier for a stored datum. In the real vault this is the content-addressed key; here it is an opaque string so the crate stays storage-agnostic.

symbols.name: ProvRecord
symbols.signature: struct ProvRecord { id: RecordId, source: String, measured_at: EpochMillis, method: MeasurementMethod, code: Option<String>, concept: String, value: f64, unit: String, reference_range: Option<ReferenceRange>, confidence: Confidence }
symbols.doc: A single provenance-tagged datum. This is the schema ADR-005 mandates be attached to **every** value at ingestion and held immutable thereafter.

symbols.name: range_position
symbols.doc: Position of this record's value against its own reference range, if any.

symbols.name: range_position
symbols.doc: Position of this record's value against its own reference range, if any.

symbols.name: DraftClaim
symbols.signature: struct DraftClaim { text: String, cites: Vec<RecordId> }
symbols.doc: A claim drafted by the analyst (ADR-002 FM-Analyst) that asserts something and *names* the records it believes back it. It is "ungrounded" until [`ground`] has verified those records exist in the retrieved evidence set.

symbols.name: new
symbols.signature: DraftClaim::fn new(text: impl _, cites: impl _) -> Self

symbols.name: new
symbols.signature: fn new(text: impl _, cites: impl _) -> Self

symbols.name: GroundedClaim
symbols.doc: A claim that has passed grounding: every cited id resolved to a real [`ProvRecord`], and those records travel *with* the claim so the UI can render inline citations and the Verifier (ADR-008) can re-derive it.

symbols.name: evidence
symbols.doc: The records that back this claim — never empty.

symbols.name: evidence
symbols.doc: The records that back this claim — never empty.

symbols.name: min_confidence
symbols.doc: Lowest confidence across the backing evidence (the claim is only as strong as its weakest support).

symbols.name: min_confidence
symbols.doc: Lowest confidence across the backing evidence (the claim is only as strong as its weakest support).

symbols.name: GroundingError
symbols.doc: Why a draft claim failed grounding. Each variant is a reason the claim must be suppressed rather than shown — the "no backing datum → no claim" rule.

symbols.name: ground
symbols.doc: The grounding gate. Returns a [`GroundedClaim`] only if **every** cited id resolves to a record in `evidence`; otherwise the claim is rejected.

symbols.name: helix_refranges
symbols.doc: # helix-refranges — population reference intervals (NHANES-derived FALLBACK)

symbols.name: RefInterval
symbols.signature: struct RefInterval { loinc: &str, name: &str, unit: &str, low: f64, high: f64, median: f64 }
symbols.doc: One population reference interval, keyed by LOINC code.

symbols.name: SOURCE
symbols.doc: Provenance string that must accompany any use of these intervals.

symbols.name: NHANES_REF
symbols.doc: NHANES 2021–2023 population reference intervals (2.5–97.5 pctile, adults 18+). Generated by `helix-bioage` example `nhanes_refranges`.

symbols.name: lookup
symbols.doc: Look up the population reference interval for a LOINC code.

symbols.name: reference_range_for
symbols.doc: Build a [`ReferenceRange`] fallback for a LOINC code (for records that arrived without one). Returns `None` if the code isn't covered.

symbols.name: position_for
symbols.doc: Classify a value against the population interval for a code (fallback only).

symbols.name: coverage
symbols.doc: How many analytes the fallback table covers.

symbols.name: helix_retrieval
symbols.doc: # helix-retrieval — ADR-023: semantic retrieval over the health graph

symbols.name: RetrievalReason
symbols.doc: Why a record was pulled into the candidate set — surfaced for audit (ADR-005) and for the Verifier (ADR-008).

symbols.name: Retrieved
symbols.signature: struct Retrieved { record: ProvRecord, score: f64, reason: RetrievalReason }
symbols.doc: A retrieved candidate: the record, its fused score, and why it was retrieved.

symbols.name: Embedder
symbols.doc: Computes a query/record embedding. RuVector's on-device encoder implements this; tests use a deterministic stub.

symbols.name: Index
symbols.doc: A vector index over the dossier. RuVector HNSW implements this.

symbols.name: neighbours
symbols.doc: Cosine-or-similar similarity neighbours of `query_vec`, as (record, sim in `[0,1]`), already capped to a reasonable candidate pool by the engine.

symbols.name: graph_links
symbols.doc: Records reachable by one graph hop from any of `seed` (clinical edges).

symbols.name: Query
symbols.signature: struct Query { text: &str, concept_codes: &[String], now: EpochMillis, top_k: usize, recency_weight: f64 }
symbols.doc: Retrieval query parameters.

symbols.name: retrieve
symbols.signature: fn retrieve(query: &Query<'_>, all_records: &[ProvRecord], embedder: &dyn _, index: &dyn _) -> Result<Vec<Retrieved>, RetrievalError>
symbols.doc: Run retrieval: direct matches + vector neighbours + one graph hop, fused, deduplicated by record id, recency-blended, and truncated to `top_k`.

symbols.name: helix_score
symbols.doc: # helix-score — ADR-016: Transparent, Decomposable, Non-Diagnostic Health Score

symbols.name: METHODOLOGY_VERSION
symbols.doc: Methodology version — bumped whenever weights or sub-score math change, so a score is always interpretable against a known, comparable formula.

symbols.name: Trend
symbols.doc: Trend direction for a contributing measurement (mirrors helix-numeric).

symbols.name: Driver
symbols.signature: struct Driver { concept: String, points: f64, trend: Trend, source_record: String }
symbols.doc: A single measurement's contribution to a sub-score, fully attributed.

symbols.name: Subsystem
symbols.signature: enum Subsystem { Cardiometabolic, Sleep, Inflammation, Fitness }
symbols.doc: The named subsystems the top-line score decomposes into.

symbols.name: SubScore
symbols.signature: struct SubScore { subsystem: Subsystem, value: f64, weight: f64, confidence: f64, drivers: Vec<Driver>, trend: Trend }
symbols.doc: A computed sub-score for one subsystem, with its drivers and confidence.

symbols.name: HealthScore
symbols.signature: struct HealthScore { value: f64, methodology_version: String, confidence: f64, subscores: Vec<SubScore>, disclaimer: String }
symbols.doc: The composite 0–100 score plus its full decomposition. The number never stands alone — it always carries the sub-scores that produced it.

symbols.name: ScoreError
symbols.signature: enum ScoreError { Empty, ValueOutOfRange, WeightOutOfRange, ConfidenceOutOfRange, ZeroWeight, NonFinite }

symbols.name: compose
symbols.doc: Roll subsystem sub-scores into the composite. The composite is a weight-normalized average of sub-score values; overall confidence is the same weighting applied to per-subsystem confidence. Deterministic and auditable — given the sub-scores, anyone can recompute the top-line numb

symbols.name: overall_trend
symbols.doc: Overall trend of the composite: improving if the weighted majority of subsystems are improving, etc. Surfaced so "which way am I heading?" is answerable at a glance (the trend-first framing of ADR-016).

symbols.name: helix_sensing
symbols.doc: # helix-sensing — ADR-020: RuView WiFi-CSI contactless ambient sensing

symbols.name: SCREENING_DISCLAIMER
symbols.doc: Framing that must accompany any RuView-derived signal.

symbols.name: CsiVitals
symbols.signature: struct CsiVitals { breathing_bpm: Option<f64>, heart_rate_bpm: Option<f64> }
symbols.doc: Vitals extracted on-device from CSI. Either may be absent for a given reading.

symbols.name: SemanticState
symbols.signature: enum SemanticState { SomeoneSleeping, RoomActive, PossibleDistress, ElderlyInactivityAnomaly, MeetingInProgress, BathroomOccupied, FallRiskElevated, BedExit, NoMovement, MultiRoomTransition, ApneaScreening }
symbols.doc: RuView's inferred semantic states (the "10 semantic states" per node).

symbols.name: escalation_level
symbols.doc: Severity this state escalates to (ADR-009). Most states are ambient context (no escalation); only safety/health-relevant ones escalate, and even then as screening prompts, never diagnoses.

symbols.name: escalation_level
symbols.doc: Severity this state escalates to (ADR-009). Most states are ambient context (no escalation); only safety/health-relevant ones escalate, and even then as screening prompts, never diagnoses.

symbols.name: RuViewReading
symbols.signature: struct RuViewReading { node_id: String, room: String, recorded_at: EpochMillis, vitals: CsiVitals, states: Vec<SemanticState>, witness_signature: String }
symbols.doc: A single RuView reading from one node, as exported by the edge mesh.

symbols.name: ScreeningFlag
symbols.signature: struct ScreeningFlag { state: SemanticState, room: String, level: EscalationLevel, message: String }
symbols.doc: A screening flag derived from a semantic state, ready for the Escalation Guardian (ADR-009).

symbols.name: reading_to_records
symbols.doc: Map a RuView reading's vitals into provenance records. Rejects unsigned or empty readings; only present, finite vitals are emitted.

symbols.name: screening_flags
symbols.doc: Map a reading's semantic states to Escalation Guardian screening flags. Only states with a non-`None` severity are returned.

symbols.name: helix_timeline
symbols.doc: # helix-timeline — ADR-031: longitudinal health-score timeline

symbols.name: Snapshot
symbols.signature: struct Snapshot { at: i64, subscores: Vec<SubScore> }
symbols.doc: A dated snapshot of the subsystem sub-scores (the inputs available at `at`).

symbols.name: ScorePoint
symbols.signature: struct ScorePoint { at: i64, value: f64, confidence: f64, methodology_version: String }
symbols.doc: One computed point on the score timeline.

symbols.name: Timeline
symbols.signature: struct Timeline { points: Vec<ScorePoint>, direction: TrendDirection, slope_per_day: Option<f64>, change_point_at: Option<i64> }
symbols.doc: The timeline plus its trend summary.

symbols.name: build_timeline
symbols.signature: fn build_timeline(snapshots: Vec<Snapshot>, flat_band: f64) -> Result<Timeline, TimelineError>
symbols.doc: Build a score timeline from dated snapshots. Each snapshot composes to one point (ADR-016); the series gets a deterministic trend + change-point. `flat_band` is the score-points/day dead-band below which the trend is flat.

symbols.name: helix_vault
symbols.doc: # helix-vault — ADR-001/013: User-Owned, Local-First Encrypted Vault

symbols.name: SealKey
symbols.doc: A 256-bit vault key. Zeroized on drop so it does not linger in memory. The user owns this; the company-side store never does.

symbols.name: from_bytes
symbols.doc: Construct from raw bytes (e.g. derived from a passphrase via a KDF — the KDF itself lives a layer up).

symbols.name: from_bytes
symbols.doc: Construct from raw bytes (e.g. derived from a passphrase via a KDF — the KDF itself lives a layer up).

symbols.name: generate
symbols.doc: Generate a fresh random key from the OS CSPRNG.

symbols.name: generate
symbols.doc: Generate a fresh random key from the OS CSPRNG.

symbols.name: SealedRecord
symbols.doc: An opaque, encrypted record. Serializable for at-rest storage / sync, but the plaintext is unrecoverable without the user's [`SealKey`].

symbols.name: len
symbols.doc: Size of the ciphertext (for storage accounting). Reveals nothing.

symbols.name: len
symbols.doc: Size of the ciphertext (for storage accounting). Reveals nothing.

symbols.name: VaultError
symbols.signature: enum VaultError { Rng, SealFailed, OpenFailed, NotFound }

symbols.name: seal
symbols.doc: Encrypt `plaintext` under `key` with a fresh random nonce.

symbols.name: open
symbols.doc: Decrypt and authenticate a [`SealedRecord`] under `key`. Fails if the key is wrong or the ciphertext was modified.

symbols.name: VaultStore
symbols.doc: The company-side store. Holds **only** ciphertext, keyed by record id. By construction it has no `SealKey` and exposes no plaintext accessor — so even a full dump of this store (breach, subpoena, bankruptcy sale) yields nothing readable. That is ADR-001 as a type, not a promise.

symbols.name: put
symbols.signature: VaultStore::fn put(self: &mut Self, id: RecordId, sealed: SealedRecord)

symbols.name: put
symbols.signature: fn put(self: &mut Self, id: RecordId, sealed: SealedRecord)

symbols.name: get
symbols.doc: Fetch the *sealed* bytes. There is deliberately no `get_plaintext`.

symbols.name: get
symbols.doc: Fetch the *sealed* bytes. There is deliberately no `get_plaintext`.

symbols.name: UserKeyring
symbols.doc: The user's keyring — held on-device only. The single capability that can turn the store's ciphertext back into plaintext (ADR-013 keeps this local).

symbols.name: seal_into
symbols.signature: UserKeyring::fn seal_into(self: &Self, store: &mut VaultStore, id: RecordId, plaintext: &[u8]) -> Result<(), VaultError>
symbols.doc: Seal a record and place it in the store.

symbols.name: seal_into
symbols.signature: fn seal_into(self: &Self, store: &mut VaultStore, id: RecordId, plaintext: &[u8]) -> Result<(), VaultError>
symbols.doc: Seal a record and place it in the store.

symbols.name: open_from
symbols.signature: UserKeyring::fn open_from(self: &Self, store: &VaultStore, id: &RecordId) -> Result<Vec<u8>, VaultError>
symbols.doc: The only path from store ciphertext back to plaintext.

symbols.name: open_from
symbols.signature: fn open_from(self: &Self, store: &VaultStore, id: &RecordId) -> Result<Vec<u8>, VaultError>
symbols.doc: The only path from store ciphertext back to plaintext.

symbols.name: helix_verifier
symbols.doc: # helix-verifier — ADR-008: Verifier/Critic Agent + Swarm Consensus

symbols.name: ModelFamily
symbols.doc: The model family that produced (or is checking) a claim. The fusion rule requires synthesizer ≠ verifier family.

symbols.name: Criticality
symbols.doc: How meaningful a claim is — drives whether full verification + consensus is required (ADR-008 tunes cost: informational claims get a lighter touch).

symbols.name: ClaimVerdict
symbols.signature: struct ClaimVerdict { attribution_ok: bool, tier_ok: bool, justified_tier: EvidenceTier, rationale: String }
symbols.doc: One verifier's structured verdict on a claim (the `ClaimVerdict` of ADR-008).

symbols.name: ClaimChecker
symbols.doc: Pluggable claim checker — in production this wraps a different-family LLM; in tests it is a deterministic stub.

symbols.name: check
symbols.signature: fn check(self: &Self, claim: &GroundedClaim, evidence: &[ProvRecord], asserted_tier: EvidenceTier) -> ClaimVerdict
symbols.doc: Re-derive the claim against the evidence and asserted tier.

symbols.name: Verification
symbols.doc: Final disposition after verification (+ consensus for clinical claims).

symbols.name: verify
symbols.signature: fn verify(claim: &GroundedClaim, evidence: &[ProvRecord], asserted_tier: EvidenceTier, synthesizer: &ModelFamily, checkers: &[&dyn _], criticality: Criticality, quorum: usize) -> Result<Verification, VerifierError>
symbols.doc: Verify a single claim. `synthesizer` is the family that drafted it; every checker must be a different family (the fusion rule). For [`Criticality:: Clinical`] claims, `quorum` independent checkers vote and a strict majority must support; informational claims need a single support

symbols.name: helix_vision
symbols.doc: # helix-vision — ADR-028: learned visual encoder (local GPU)

symbols.name: LAYOUT_PROMPT
symbols.doc: The layout-only vision prompt (ADR-028 §1). Asks for type/layout, forbids content.

symbols.name: NEUTRAL_TOKEN
symbols.doc: A neutral fallback when the caption is rejected by the value-guard.

symbols.name: VisionCaptioner
symbols.doc: Produces a layout-only caption for an image (PNG bytes). Production: a GPU vision model; tests: a deterministic stub.

symbols.name: caption_is_layout_only
symbols.doc: True if `caption` is safe (layout-only): no digit means it did not read a value.

symbols.name: base64
symbols.doc: Minimal, dependency-free base64 (standard alphabet) for embedding image bytes in the vision request.

symbols.name: LayoutVisualEncoder
symbols.signature: struct LayoutVisualEncoder { captioner: C, embedder: E }
symbols.doc: The learned visual encoder: caption (layout-only, guarded) → embed.

symbols.name: VisualEncoding
symbols.signature: struct VisualEncoding { caption: String, embedding: Vec<f32>, guarded: bool }
symbols.doc: What an encode produced — the (guarded) caption and its embedding.

symbols.name: new
symbols.signature: LayoutVisualEncoder::fn new(captioner: C, embedder: E) -> Self

symbols.name: new
symbols.signature: fn new(captioner: C, embedder: E) -> Self

symbols.name: encode
symbols.doc: Encode an image: caption it layout-only, enforce the value-guard, embed.

symbols.name: encode
symbols.doc: Encode an image: caption it layout-only, enforce the value-guard, embed.

symbols.name: VisualMatch
symbols.signature: struct VisualMatch { doc_id: String, score: f32, caption: String }
symbols.doc: A visual retrieval match (a similar-LOOKING document, never an interpretation).

symbols.name: rank
symbols.signature: fn rank(query: &VisualEncoding, corpus: &[(String, VisualEncoding)], k: usize) -> Vec<VisualMatch>
symbols.doc: Rank a corpus of encodings against a query encoding by cosine similarity.

symbols.name: OllamaVision
symbols.signature: struct OllamaVision { url: String, model: String, timeout_secs: u64 }
symbols.doc: GPU vision backend: an ollama vision model (e.g. `moondream`) on the local GPU.

symbols.name: helix_visual
symbols.doc: # helix-visual — ADR-025: visual RAG over medical documents & images

symbols.name: Gray
symbols.signature: struct Gray { w: u32, h: u32, px: Vec<u8> }
symbols.doc: A grayscale image (row-major luminance, 0..=255).

symbols.name: new
symbols.signature: Gray::fn new(w: u32, h: u32, px: Vec<u8>) -> Result<Self, VisualError>

symbols.name: new
symbols.signature: fn new(w: u32, h: u32, px: Vec<u8>) -> Result<Self, VisualError>

symbols.name: from_rgba
symbols.signature: Gray::fn from_rgba(w: u32, h: u32, rgba: &[u8]) -> Result<Self, VisualError>
symbols.doc: Build from interleaved RGBA bytes (e.g. a decoded PNG) via Rec.601 luma.

symbols.name: from_rgba
symbols.signature: fn from_rgba(w: u32, h: u32, rgba: &[u8]) -> Result<Self, VisualError>
symbols.doc: Build from interleaved RGBA bytes (e.g. a decoded PNG) via Rec.601 luma.

symbols.name: DocEmbedding
symbols.signature: struct DocEmbedding { tiles: Vec<Vec<f32>>, grid: u32, cells: u32 }
symbols.doc: One document's multi-vector embedding: one descriptor per tile.

symbols.name: VisualEmbedder
symbols.doc: Pluggable visual embedder. The in-crate [`PerceptualEmbedder`] is the reference; a learned ColPali/MiniLM encoder plugs in here.

symbols.name: PerceptualEmbedder
symbols.signature: struct PerceptualEmbedder { grid: u32, cells: u32 }
symbols.doc: Configuration for the reference perceptual embedder.

symbols.name: maxsim
symbols.doc: ColPali MaxSim late interaction: for each query tile, its best cosine over the document's tiles, averaged. Clamped to `[0,1]`. Requires matching descriptor shape (same grid/cells).

symbols.name: VisualMatch
symbols.signature: struct VisualMatch { doc_id: String, score: f32, note: String }
symbols.doc: A visual retrieval result — a *similar-looking* document, never an interpretation.

symbols.name: VisualIndex
symbols.doc: An index of document embeddings. RuVector HNSW/IVF caps the candidate set before MaxSim in production; here we score the corpus directly.

symbols.name: add
symbols.signature: VisualIndex::fn add(self: &mut Self, id: impl _, emb: DocEmbedding)

symbols.name: add
symbols.signature: fn add(self: &mut Self, id: impl _, emb: DocEmbedding)

symbols.name: retrieve
symbols.signature: VisualIndex::fn retrieve(self: &Self, query: &DocEmbedding, k: usize) -> Result<Vec<VisualMatch>, VisualError>
symbols.doc: Rank documents by MaxSim against `query`, return the top `k`.

symbols.name: retrieve
symbols.signature: fn retrieve(self: &Self, query: &DocEmbedding, k: usize) -> Result<Vec<VisualMatch>, VisualError>
symbols.doc: Rank documents by MaxSim against `query`, return the top `k`.

symbols.name: helix_wasm
symbols.doc: # helix-wasm — the WebAssembly surface of the Helix analytic core

symbols.name: AnalyzePayload
symbols.signature: struct AnalyzePayload { concept_code: String, records: Vec<ProvRecord>, now: i64, staleness_window_days: i64, confidence_floor: f64, reference_low: Option<f64>, reference_high: Option<f64>, flat_band_per_day: f64, flat_band_frac: f64 }
symbols.doc: The owned mirror of `helix_pipeline::AnalyzeRequest` (which borrows). Deserialized from the JS payload, then turned into the borrowed request internally.

symbols.name: analyze_json
symbols.doc: Run the grounded-answer pipeline. Input: an [`AnalyzePayload`] as JSON. Output: a `helix_pipeline::AnswerOutcome` as JSON (`abstained` or `answered`).

symbols.name: compose_score_json
symbols.doc: Compose a decomposable 0–100 health score. Input: an array of `SubScore` as JSON. Output: a `HealthScore` as JSON.

symbols.name: redflag_registry_version
symbols.doc: The red-flag threshold registry version currently in force (ADR-009).

symbols.name: neural_session_to_records_json
symbols.doc: Ingest a `ruv-neural` signed session (JSON) and return the provenance-tagged records it maps to (JSON array), so EEG/40 Hz entrainment signals flow into the same dossier as labs — as a research/screening signal (ADR-014 framing).

symbols.name: neural_disclaimer
symbols.doc: The non-diagnostic disclaimer that must accompany any ruv-neural signal.

symbols.name: sensing_reading_json
symbols.doc: Ingest a RuView WiFi-CSI reading (ADR-020): returns `{records, flags}` — vital ProvRecords plus Escalation Guardian screening flags.

symbols.name: genome_profile_json
symbols.doc: Ingest a user-owned genome profile (ADR-021): returns `{records, advisories}` — GENO-* records plus "verify with your prescriber" pharmacogenomic advisories.

symbols.name: ocr_ingest_json
symbols.signature: fn ocr_ingest_json(document: &str, floor: f64) -> Result<String, JsValue>
symbols.doc: Gate an OCR'd lab document (ADR-022): returns the gated outcomes (accepted records / queued candidates with reasons). `floor` is the minimum OCR confidence to accept.

symbols.name: bioage_json
symbols.doc: Biological-age estimate (ADR-034): input PhenoInputs JSON → BioAge + disclaimer.

symbols.name: focus_json
symbols.doc: Focus areas (ADR-032): input `{records, now, config}` JSON → ranked focus items.

symbols.name: timeline_json
symbols.doc: Score timeline (ADR-031): input `{snapshots, flat_band}` JSON → versioned ScorePoints + trend + change-point.

symbols.name: fhir_import_json
symbols.doc: Import a FHIR R4 Bundle (ADR-029): parse every `Observation` entry into provenance records. Returns `{records, queued}` — un-parseable resources are counted into the review queue (ADR-012), never silently dropped.

symbols.name: population_range_json
symbols.doc: Population reference interval (NHANES-derived fallback) for a LOINC code. Returns `{low, high, median, name, unit, source}` or `null` if not covered. FALLBACK only — never overrides a lab's own range (ADR-006 tiering).

symbols.name: population_range_coverage
symbols.doc: Number of analytes the population-reference fallback covers.

symbols.name: apple_health_import_json
symbols.doc: Import an Apple Health `export.xml` (ADR-029): parse known HealthKit records into provenance records. Bounded to 100k records. Returns the records JSON.

symbols.name: genome_raw_import_json
symbols.doc: Import a 23andMe-style raw genotype file (ADR-021): surfaces a few documented single-SNP findings (NOT a full diplotype call). Returns the RawGenomeResult.

symbols.name: visual_encode_json
symbols.signature: fn visual_encode_json(w: u32, h: u32, px: &[u8]) -> Result<String, JsValue>
symbols.doc: Visual encode (ADR-025/028): grayscale pixels (row-major, w*h bytes) → the perceptual tile embedding (DocEmbedding JSON). For OCR/visual previews.

symbols.name: visual_maxsim_json
symbols.doc: Visual similarity (ADR-025): MaxSim between two DocEmbeddings (JSON) → score.

symbols.name: version
symbols.doc: Crate version string for the UI footer / diagnostics.
